metadata.title: How to Deal With Insurance After a Kansas Accident
metadata.description: Learn how to deal with insurance after a Kansas accident, avoid common mistakes, understand your rights, and protect your injury claim.
keywords: how to deal with insurance after accident Kansas

hero.label: KANSAS INSURANCE GUIDANCE
hero.title: How to Deal With Insurance After an Accident in Kansas

author.name: Bradley A. Pistotnik (Trial Lawyer)
author.bio: Brad Pistotnik is the CEO of Bull Attorneys®, P.A. and a Kansas trucking and personal injury lawyer with over 45 years of experience. He focuses on serious injury and truck accident cases involving DOT regulations and commercial vehicle safety standards.

sections.content: After a crash the insurance company may contact you quickly. Stay calm, keep good records, and avoid fast decisions before you understand your injuries and your rights.

items.heading: Get Medical Care First
items.text: Your health comes first. Early treatment protects your recovery and creates a record of your injuries. Don't ignore symptoms just because you want to get back to normal.

items.heading: Report the Accident Carefully
items.text: Stick to the facts. Be honest. Don't guess. Don't downplay your injuries. What you say early in a claim can affect how the insurance company handles your case later.

items.heading: Be Careful With Recorded Statements
items.text: We warn people not to give recorded statements to adjusters right after a crash — many people don't know how badly they're hurt in the first 24 hours.

sections.label: K.S.A. 60-2801
sections.heading: Kansas Has a 14-Day Protection Rule
sections.paragraphHtml: Within 14 days of an injury event, a party whose interest may be adverse to the injured person cannot negotiate a settlement or try to get a general release while the injured person is under medical care or in a hospital.

timeline.label: THE FIRST 14 DAYS AFTER A KANSAS CRASH

items.label: Day 0
items.text: Get medical help, call police, take photos.

items.label: Days 1–3
items.text: Start your insurance file and track symptoms.

items.label: Days 1–14
items.text: Be cautious about settlement talks and releases.

sections.label: STAY ORGANIZED
sections.heading: Keep a Simple Insurance File
sections.intro: The easiest way to stay organized is to keep one file for your claim. Good records help you avoid confusion and support your claim.
gridItems: Notes from phone calls and emails

alertCard.heading: Watch Out for Quick Settlement Pressure
paragraphs: Insurance companies may move fast — that doesn't always mean they're trying to help you. A fast settlement may not cover future treatment, lost wages, or ongoing pain.
Slow down, get treatment, understand your injuries, and ask questions before agreeing to anything.

items.label: Kansas protection period in certain injury-settlement situations
People injured every half hour in distracted-driving accidents (NHTSA)
All your claim records in one place

sections.heading: When to Get Legal Help
sections.intro: The more serious the crash, the more important it is to understand your options before signing anything.
items: A truck or company vehicle was involved
The insurance company is pressuring you

faqs.question: What should I do first when dealing with insurance after an accident?
faqs.answer: Get medical care first, report the crash carefully, keep good records, and avoid making quick decisions before you understand your injuries and your rights.

faqs.question: Should I give a recorded statement to the insurance company right away?
faqs.answer: You should be careful. Many people do not know how badly they are hurt in the first 24 hours after a crash, and early statements may create problems later.

faqs.question: What is Kansas' 14-day protection rule after an injury accident?
faqs.answer: Under K.S.A. 60-2801, within 14 days of the injury event, a person whose interests may be adverse to the injured person cannot negotiate a settlement or try to get a general release if the injured person is under care or in a hospital.

faqs.question: What records should I keep after an accident?
faqs.answer: Keep your claim number, adjuster name, medical bills, repair estimates, photos, wage-loss records, pharmacy costs, and notes about calls or emails related to the claim.

faqs.question: When should I get legal help with an insurance claim?
faqs.answer: You may want legal help if you have serious injuries, the insurance company is pressuring you, fault is disputed, a truck was involved, or a loved one died in the crash.

schema.alternativeHeadline: Kansas Insurance Claim Guide After a Car or Truck Accident
schema.description: After a crash in Kansas, the insurance company may contact you quickly. Learn how to stay organized, protect your rights, and avoid costly mistakes.
keywords: how to deal with insurance after accident Kansas
schema.articleBody: After a car accident in Kansas, the insurance company may contact you quickly. You should get medical care first, report the crash carefully, and avoid giving recorded statements right away. Kansas has a 14-day protection rule under K.S.A. 60-2801 — within 14 days of an injury event, the other party cannot negotiate a settlement while you are under medical care. Keep a simple insurance file with your claim number, adjuster name, medical bills, repair estimates, photos, and notes. Be cautious about quick settlement pressure — a fast offer may not cover future treatment or lost wages. Get legal help if you have serious injuries, a truck was involved, fault is disputed, or you are being pressured to settle early.

disclaimer: This blog provides general information only, not legal advice. Reading it does not create an attorney-client relationship. Every case is different. Results vary depending on each case's facts and legal circumstances. Deadlines and laws can change, so speak directly with an attorney about your accident.
